exemplar.title: In what ways does changing the velocity of air contacting an isolated flat plate wing affect the lift force generated when placed in a wind tunnel?

detailedExemplarData.title: In what ways does changing the velocity of air contacting an isolated flat plate wing affect the lift force generated when placed in a wind tunnel?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables are meticulously organized with units, uncertainties and statistical columns, ensuring clarity.
Graphs include error bars, fitted functions and clear labels, supporting precise interpretation.
Linearisation of vÂ² yields a robust linear relationship, facilitating accurate slope analysis.
Gradient and intercept uncertainties are propagated correctly and compared to theoretical values.
Time-series plots of lift support the choice of averaging interval and data stability.
weaknesses: Inconsistent notation Fi vs F_L and missing units on some axes reduce precision.
Uncertainties of density and surface area are omitted from propagation, leading to incomplete error analysis.
No formal statistical criterion is provided for outlier identification when discarding negative readings.
The mean calculation example mislabels units, indicating a lapse in unit consistency.
Outlier-rejection and averaging window choices lack statistical justification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data recording and processing is both clear and precise overall, and the major calculations (averaging, linearisation, uncertainty propagation of gradient/intercept) are executed accurately. Some significant omissionsâparticularly in uncertainty propagation of auxiliary variables and formal outlier handlingâpreclude a full band-6 score but confidently place the work at band 5.

strengths: The research question clearly frames key variables within a specific aerodynamic context, citing Bernoulli and Newtonian principles.
Precise specification of discrete velocities, angle of attack and lift force enhances clarity and reproducibility.
Control variables are succinctly summarized with clear cross-referencing (Section 1.2), avoiding redundancy.
Figure 2 provides a detailed, labeled illustration of the experimental setup, aiding reproducibility.
weaknesses: Background discussion lacks quantitative linkage of Bernoulli/Newton theories to flat-plate behavior in this setup.
Choice of a flat plate versus cambered foil is only briefly justified without in-depth rationale.
Assumed humidity control is not verified by direct measurement or uncertainty estimate.
Procedure for trial timing omits duration and stabilization intervals, hindering exact reproduction.
Anemometer alignment and calibration procedures are not fully detailed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student situates the research question within a well-defined aerodynamic context and provides a mostly complete methodology, meriting a band-5 overall. Minor omissionsâparticularly in justifying foil choice, humidity verification and precise timingâprevent a perfect score, but the work is coherent, specific and largely reproducible.

strengths: The conclusion restates the proportional relationship between F_L and vÂ² with its uncertainty and aligns fully with the analysis.
Comparison of the experimentally determined constant k to NASAâs theoretical value is well justified and shows overlap within uncertainty.
Discussion identifies both systematic and random error sources, linking back to experimental steps.
References to stalling limits and flat-plate theory reinforce relevance to accepted scientific context.
weaknesses: Discussion of precision versus accuracy is brief and could be expanded.
No mention of Reynolds number or flow-regime considerations to deepen scientific context.
Could enrich the scientific context by discussing the implications for real-world aerodynamic applications.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is fully consistent with the data analysis and supports the research question with appropriate scientific context, satisfying the requirements for a band-5 conclusion. Minor elaborations (e.g., Reynolds number discussion) would be needed for a top band-6 justification.

strengths: Identifies specific methodological weaknesses (turbulence, sensor resolution, anemometer uncertainty) with clear evidence.
Proposes realistic improvements (e.g., laminar-flow fan, higher-precision sensors) directly tied to each cited weakness.
Explains how each suggested improvement would reduce the corresponding uncertainty, showing good causal linkage.
Addresses both random and systematic error reduction in a coherent manner.
weaknesses: Explanations of impact remain qualitative without quantitative estimates (e.g., percent error reduction).
Practical constraints (cost, equipment availability, calibration requirements) are not discussed.
Could strengthen evaluation by incorporating simple flow-regime analysis or Reynolds number estimation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student goes beyond stating generic limitations by describing specific weaknesses and realistic, relevant improvements. Each improvement is causally linked to a methodological issue, approaching the band-5 descriptor. Although deeper quantification of impact would strengthen the evaluation, the quality of reasoning justifies raising this criterion to a 5.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student frames the question within a clear aerodynamics context, citing Bernoulli, Newton and stalling limits, and specifies the angle of attack, fluid, and surface geometry.  The background discussion explicitly links the variables to the lift-equation and justifies the chosen velocity range, giving the reader a precise situational picture rather than a vague overview.  Minor gaps (e.g. no brief justification for flat-plate versus cambered foil) keep the work from a perfect 6, but the context remains specific and appropriate.

Instrument uncertainties are quoted, combined for vÂ², and propagated to gradient and intercept.  Nevertheless, density and surface-area uncertainties are ignored, and propagation through the mean FL values is asserted rather than calculated.  These significant omissions justify the middle (3-4) band; the depth displayed merits the upper end of that band (4).

Methodological considerations are explained rather than merely listed.  The student discusses the role and control of density, angle of attack, and surface area, cites the anemometerâs percentage error, and links each variable to lift theory.  They also justify a five-level velocity range and replicate force readings five times.  However, some explanations (e.g. classroom humidity assumed) show slight superficiality, so a solid 5 rather than 6 is appropriate.

Three specific weaknesses (turbulent flow, force-sensor resolution, anemometer uncertainty) are described with evidence and their impact on data quality (error bars, oscillations).  The explanation of impact is competent, though depth (e.g., quantitative estimation of error contribution) is limited, fitting the descriptor for 3-4 band; clarity merits the upper score of 4.

The step-by-step procedure, photographs and equipment list allow the investigation to be reproduced with only a few ambiguities (fanâtoâmodel distance is adjusted âuntilâ a speed is reached, but the exact distances are not logged; the time window used for averaging is only described qualitatively).  Instrument resolutions and uncertainties are tabulated, yet the method for positioning the anemometer and maintaining its orientation is not fully detailed.  Hence reproduction is possible with minor guessworkâconsistent with the 3-4 band.

Averages, linearisation, gradient and intercept uncertainties are correctly executed; the gradient is compared to a theoretical constant with agreement inside uncertainty.  Calculations are internally consistent, and no major errors are evident.  Minor rounding inconsistencies prevent a 6, so 5 is awarded.

Improvements (laminar-flow fan, larger velocity range, higher-precision anemometer) are realistic and directly address the cited weaknesses.  The student explains how each improvement would reduce specific uncertainties, though explanations remain qualitative.  This satisfies the âdescribedâ level (3-4) and is closer to a 4 than a 3 due to clear linkage between weakness and remedy.

Data tables are neatly organized with units, uncertainties and statistical columns; graphs are labelled, include error bars and fitted functions.  A minor formatting slip (use of Fi vs FL) prevents absolute precision, so 5/6 reflects work that is both clear and largely precise.

The student compares slope and intercept to NASAâs lift equation, comments on systematic error absence, and identifies random error sources.  References to stalling limits and flat-plate theory reinforce alignment with accepted scientific context.  Depth is substantial but not exhaustive (e.g., Reynolds number discussion absent), warranting 5/6.

The conclusion restates the quantitative relationship, cites the experimentally obtained constant with its uncertainty and explicitly links it to the theoretical value from NASA.  The reasoning is entirely consistent with the analysis, but the discussion of precision versus accuracy remains brief, so a 5 accurately reflects strong but not exemplary justification.

annotations.comment: The research question clearly frames key variables (velocity range, angle of attack, flat plate geometry) within an aerodynamic context, enhancing the specificity and appropriateness of the investigation scope.
Equation (2) is presented with all relevant variables and units, making the theoretical framework clear and reproducible.
The research question statement precisely specifies discrete velocities, attack angle, and lift force, aiding clarity and allowing reproducibility.

The Bernoulli and Newtonian background is presented but lacks critical linkage to flat-plate behavior in this specific setup; consider discussing how these theories quantitatively inform expected lift values.
The choice to apply the Wesseinger Approximation to a flat plate is noted, but the potential deviation from an ideal thin, cambered foil is only briefly justified; expand this rationale.
Controlled variables (angle, density, surface area) are summarized but the humidity control assumes a fixed value; describe how humidity was monitored or measured.
The summary note on control variables efficiently references Section 1.2, demonstrating logical organization and avoiding redundancy.
Figure 2 clearly illustrates the experimental setup with labeled components, enhancing the reproducibility of the procedure.
The description of trial timing (âturning fan on and off until 5 readingsâ) omits duration and stabilization intervals; specify these to ensure consistent data collection.
The raw data table is well organized with statistical and instrumental uncertainties clearly presented, aiding clarity.
Figure 1 is informative but the caption and discussion do not explicitly connect the inclination effects shown to the flat-plate experimental design; clarify this link.
The air density calculation employs assumed humidity without quantifying its uncertainty; include an uncertainty estimate for density to strengthen methodological rigor.
Surface area is stated as 0.007 mÂ² but its measurement uncertainty is not given; record and propagate the areaâs uncertainty in subsequent calculations.
The method for measuring plate dimensions lacks procedural detail (e.g., orientation, measurement points); specify how the ruler was positioned and what points were measured.
Closing the door to control humidity is described, but no direct humidity measurement is reported; include the instrument or method used to verify ambient humidity.
Table 1 thoroughly lists instrument uncertainties but the procedure for achieving intermediate velocities (âvarying the distanceâ) is vague and hinders exact reproduction.
Anemometer positioning (3 cm in front) is noted but sensor alignment and calibration are not described; detail how the device was oriented and stabilized.
No formal criterion for identifying outliers is provided when discarding negative readings; define a statistical test or threshold for outlier exclusion.
Uncertainty propagation for vÂ² is only described qualitatively; include the full propagation formula to justify the Â±6%.
Axes in Figure 5 lack explicit unit labels; add units to each axis to improve precision and clarity.
Before linearization, the pure results section could quantify the trend (e.g., approximate exponent) to demonstrate the quadratic expectation.
Notation for velocity (âÎ½â) appears inconsistently italicized in the introductory text; standardize formatting of symbols throughout.
Linearization in Figure 6 is well executed, transforming vÂ² into a clear linear relationship and facilitating slope analysis.
The derivation of equation (3) uses underlining for variables inconsistently; align formatting with the rest of the report.
The choice to use only magnitudes of lift omits sign conventions; include a note on coordinate axes and sign interpretation for directional forces.
Uncertainties for density and surface area are not propagated into the calculated uncertainties for F_L; incorporate these to obtain a more complete error analysis.
Figure 4 effectively shows force fluctuations over time, supporting the rationale for averaging a stable interval.
A 2-second averaging window is used without justification of its representativeness; tie this choice to a stability criterion or convergence test.
The mean calculation example labels units as [m sâ»Â¹] instead of newtons; ensure correct unit reporting for lift force.
Including an RÂ² or goodness-of-fit metric on the linearized graph would quantitatively support the appropriateness of the linear model.
Gradient uncertainty calculation via maxâmin/2 is clear but could be enhanced by incorporating regression standard error from the fit.
The y-intercept uncertainty derivation lacks a step-by-step explanation; include the intercept range values and interpolation method.
The evaluation accurately identifies turbulent flow as a source of random error but lacks quantification (e.g., Reynolds number); incorporate flow regime analysis.
The suggestion to use a laminar-flow fan addresses flow instability but should consider practical constraints like cost and speed control capabilities.
Replacing the force meter with one having higher resolution is relevant; also consider calibrating the existing sensor or using a different range setting.
Percentage uncertainty is calculated correctly, but note the significant-figure rationale to justify reporting 7.89%.
The conclusion restates the proportional relationship between F_L and vÂ² clearly and is fully consistent with the analysis.
Comparing the experimentally determined constant k to NASAâs theoretical value and noting overlap within uncertainty is well justified.
Random vs systematic error discussion is good but could be enriched by commenting on Reynolds number to place turbulence in scientific context.
The overall summary is clear but slightly repetitive; consider combining the reliability and precision remarks more concisely.
Upgrading the anemometer to a device with lower uncertainty is realistic; you might also perform a pre-calibration curve to correct systematic bias.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
